turns.text: Hello Alessandra, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I appreciate you asking, I really do. It’s just… talking about it feels a bit like picking at a scab that hasn’t finished healing yet. 

Living in Salem, looking out at the cobblestones, I keep expecting to feel some kind of "new mother" peace, but the quiet just makes the memories louder. When I was pregnant, I wasn’t just a person to the doctors; I was a list of red flags. They’d come in, talk about protein levels and my blood pressure numbers, and I’d just stare at the ceiling, feeling like a ticking bomb. Being twenty-two and dealing with all of that—the pre-eclampsia, the fear—it was so incredibly isolating. I didn't have anyone to hold my hand in that delivery room, and every time someone touched me to check a monitor or move a wire, it felt like I was losing more control of my own skin.

It’s hard to put into words, but I didn’t feel like a mother-to-be. I felt like a patient they were trying to manage before something went wrong. Now, I’m home, and I’m terrified that the swelling is coming back or that my head is starting to throb again. I just wanted to be treated like a person, not a high-risk case. I'm ready to talk, I think, as long as we can take it slow.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

Honestly, I think I was so preoccupied with just surviving the delivery that I didn’t dare look past it. I had this naive, quiet hope that once the baby was out and the monitors stopped screaming, the pressure would just... vanish. I expected to feel like a mother, you know? I thought I’d be tired, sure—everyone talks about the sleepless nights—but I pictured a recovery that was physical, something I could nurse with ice packs and rest. 

The reality wasn’t like that at all. It wasn’t just about my body healing from the birth; it was this lingering, haunting dread. Every time I stood up, I’d panic, checking my feet for fluid, terrified that the pre-eclampsia was still hiding in my blood, waiting to flare up. My body felt like it didn't belong to me anymore, like it was a foreign object that had betrayed me. Instead of focusing on bonding, I was constantly scanning myself for "symptoms." 

Even now, the physical healing is overshadowed by that vigilance. It’s not just a sore stomach or exhaustion; it’s the way my heart still trips over itself if I feel a little lightheaded. I wanted a soft start to motherhood, but this feels more like I’m still running from something that already caught me.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I think, in the back of my mind, I pictured feeding as the one thing that would be simple—a quiet, soft moment where I could finally just breathe and connect without a monitor beeping or a nurse hovering. I really wanted that closeness, that feeling of finally doing something "right" after my body had been under such a microscope for months. I didn't think it would be a science project, and I certainly didn't expect it to feel like another battle.

When the time actually came, I was so depleted from everything else that I just felt hollow. My body had already been through so much, and the physical act of feeding just felt like one more demand on a tank that was already empty. The support I got felt… clinical. They’d come in, adjust my arms, check the latch, and talk about ounces and intervals, but nobody really saw that I was shaking or that the trauma of the birth made every touch feel like an intrusion. 

It wasn't the peaceful, bonding experience the brochures talk about. It was another job, another source of anxiety. I’d be sitting there, exhausted, trying to track numbers while my head was spinning with "what-ifs," feeling like if the baby didn't eat perfectly, it was just another sign that my body was failing again. It was lonely. Everything felt so lonely.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

That gap felt like a chasm. The nurses were all about the "mechanics"—the latch, the hold, the frequency. They’d come in with these checklists, ticking off boxes to make sure I was hitting the right milestones, but they never seemed to notice that I was basically dissociating while they did it. 

I didn't need someone to move my hands into the "correct" position; I needed someone to look at me—actually look at me—and ask if I was okay. I needed someone to recognize that I was a young woman who was scared to death, not just a feeding vessel. When they were so focused on the technical success of the breastfeeding, it felt like they were ignoring the person behind the breasts. It made me feel like an object, a machine that had to be calibrated properly. 

If someone had just sat with me, maybe acknowledged that this was hard or asked how the trauma of the delivery was affecting me, I might have felt human. Instead, I felt like a set of vitals. It’s hard to foster that "magical" connection when you’re being treated like a broken piece of equipment that needs to be fixed before the next shift change. They provided information, sure, but they didn't provide *care*. There’s a world of difference.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

I think I had this naive idea that the "postpartum period" would just be a transition into a new, albeit tiring, rhythm. I expected the blues, maybe some tears from hormones, but I truly believed that once I held the baby, the world would settle. I expected that the heavy, suffocating anxiety I felt during the pregnancy would just lift, like a fog clearing out over the harbor.

The reality hit me like a brick wall. It wasn't just "baby blues." It felt like I brought the hospital room home with me. Even now, weeks later, I feel like I’m constantly looking over my shoulder. Every time the baby cries, my chest tightens, and I’m back in that delivery room, reliving the fear that I was going to die right there on the table. 

I’m struggling to feel like a mother because I’m so busy being a survivor. I’m hyper-vigilant—checking the blood pressure, checking the baby, checking myself. There’s no room for joy because my brain is too busy running disaster scenarios. I feel so much younger than twenty-two right now, and I’m exhausted by how much of my energy is spent just trying not to panic. It’s not the transition I imagined; it’s more like living in the wreckage of a storm that I’m not sure has really passed yet.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

That’s a heavy question. Honestly? Yes, absolutely. There’s this unspoken script when you have a baby: you’re supposed to be glowing, tired but "fulfilled," and just so grateful for the gift of life. It’s like there’s this performance you’re expected to put on. When I was in the hospital, the nurses were so focused on the fact that we both survived that any hint of my fear or my trauma felt like I was being ungrateful. It was like, "Alessandra, you’re high-risk, you’re stable now, just be happy."

So, I bit my tongue. I didn't want to be the "difficult" patient who didn't appreciate that they kept me alive. And it’s not much better with family. People see the baby and they want to hear about how beautiful she is, not about how I’m checking my own pulse every thirty minutes because I’m terrified of a stroke. 

Even with myself, I feel this immense guilt. I feel like I’m failing at being a mother because I’m so stuck in my own head, scared of my own body. I feel like I’m not "allowed" to be grieving the birth I didn't get, because society tells you the result is all that matters. But the result isn't all that matters. My experience matters, and it was terrifying. Holding that in is just as exhausting as the lack of sleep.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

I think I expected to feel like "Alessandra, the mother"—a version of myself that was just a bit more responsible, maybe a little softer around the edges. I thought it would be an expansion of who I already was, like adding a new, beautiful chapter to a book. I wanted to be the kind of mom who felt steady and capable, someone who could be a sanctuary for my daughter.

But the reality is that I feel like the *person* I was has been pushed into the background, and in her place is this shell that’s entirely defined by medical caution. My identity now feels so brittle. I’m not just "Alessandra" anymore; I’m "the girl who had the complication." I’m "the high-risk case." Every time I look in the mirror, I don't see a woman who just gave life; I see a set of risks and vulnerabilities that everyone was trying to manage.

It feels like my identity has been hijacked by the medical system. I spend so much time monitoring my own vitals and guarding against anxiety that I’ve forgotten what it’s like to just *be*. I’m fighting to find myself again, but it’s hard when I’m still so scared that my own body is going to turn on me again. I’m not a mother who’s growing; I’m a woman who is trying to find the ground again after an earthquake.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

Everything, really. Before this, I was working, I had my own little rhythm, my own independence. I felt like I was starting to carve out a space for myself in this world. Now? That space has completely evaporated. 

My professional life feels like a lifetime ago. How am I supposed to care about deadlines or emails when I’m still paralyzed by the fear that I might have another health crisis? I feel like I’ve lost my edge, that sharp, capable part of me that handled life. Now I’m just… soft, and not in the "maternal" way—just fragile. 

Socially, it’s even harder. My friends are out there living their lives, talking about things that feel so distant from where I am. I can't relate to them anymore. If I try to talk about the terror of the delivery, the room goes quiet. People don't want to hear about the "wreck," they want to hear about the baby’s first smile. So I just stay here, in this kitchen, feeling like I’m hiding. I’ve lost that sense of being an equal participant in my own life. I feel like an observer watching someone else’s disaster, and I don't know how to step back into the world when I’m still so busy keeping myself from shattering.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those check-ups to be a place to exhale. I thought they would be the moment where someone would finally ask, "How are *you* doing, Alessandra? Not your blood pressure, not your labs—how is your heart?" I assumed there would be some kind of bridge to help me process the trauma, or at least someone to tell me that the fear I was feeling was a normal reaction to a terrifying situation.

Instead, the reality was just a continuation of the same clinical machinery. The appointments felt like efficiency audits. They’d check my incision, look at the charts, confirm the blood pressure was—thankfully—drifting down, and then clear me for "normal" life. It was all about the physical status. "You're healing well," they’d say, and I’d just nod, even though I felt like I was completely breaking down inside. 

They weren't looking for a person; they were looking for a successfully closed file. There was no space for the fear, no resources for the panic attacks, and definitely no acknowledgment that a twenty-two-year-old alone might need more than a physical exam. I left every single one of those appointments feeling smaller and more invisible than when I walked in. They checked the body, but they never checked on the woman who had to live in it.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

I tried, once. I remember sitting there—my hands wouldn't stop shaking—and I told the doctor that I couldn't sleep because I kept seeing the monitors in my head. I told her I was scared to be alone, that the panic felt like it was physically choking me. I was hoping for... I don't know, a referral, a resource, just a nod that it wasn't just in my head.

She barely looked up from her computer screen. She just sort of clicked a pen and said, "It's normal to be anxious with a newborn, Alessandra. You’ve been through a lot, but your vitals are perfect. Focus on the baby; the rest will fade."

That was it. "It's normal." As if that makes it okay. As if that makes the terror less real. By calling it "normal," she essentially dismissed it. It made me feel like I was just being dramatic, or like I was failing at the "motherhood" part by being a mess. I didn't raise it again. Why would I? If the expert tells you it’s just a normal part of the process, you start to believe that you’re just weak for not being able to handle it. I stopped being honest after that. I just gave them the answers they wanted so I could leave and get back to my house.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

I haven’t returned yet, and honestly, the thought of it is like staring at a cliff I have to jump off. I thought, months ago, that going back to work would be my anchor. I expected it to be the one place where I was just "Alessandra" again, not the patient, not the mother, just someone who could complete a task without being defined by my medical history. I imagined it would give me back a sense of competence, a reminder of the life I had before all the "high-risk" labels started pinning me down.

But now? The reality feels like I’m going to be a hollow version of myself. I’m terrified that I’ll be sitting at my desk and the familiar, sharp panic will hit—that feeling that my pulse is racing or that my vision is blurring—and I won’t be able to hide it. I’m scared that my brain is still so fogged by trauma that I won’t be able to keep up, and that if I fail at work, it’ll just confirm the feeling that my body and mind are broken. 

Work used to be an escape; now, it feels like another stage where I have to hide how fragile I really am. I’m not just worried about the schedule; I’m worried that I’ve forgotten how to be anyone other than a person who is just trying to survive.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

This is the part that hurts the most to talk about, because the "we" I imagined just isn’t there. I’m doing this alone. Before the baby arrived, I think I desperately wanted to believe that if things got scary—if the blood pressure spiked or the panic set in—there would be someone standing on the other side of that hospital bed to catch me. I expected a partner who would be my anchor, someone to hold the baby while I slept, or just hold my hand so I wouldn't have to look at the monitors alone. I thought we’d be a team, navigating the sleepless nights and the fear together.

The reality is that the silence in this apartment is deafening. Every time I hit a wall, I turn to look for someone, and there’s just no one there. It’s not just the exhaustion of the physical work; it’s the crushing weight of having to make every single decision while I’m barely keeping my own head above water. When I’m spiraling at 3:00 AM, there’s no one to tag in. I’m the only one who knows how terrified I am, and I’m the only one who can comfort the baby. It’s just me, my fear, and this tiny life that I’m so afraid I’m going to mess up because I’m so damaged.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I guess I expected maternity care to be a bridge. I thought it would be a partnership where I brought the fear and the vulnerability, and they provided the safety, the expertise, and the humanity to help me walk across to the other side—to motherhood—feeling capable and seen. I expected them to be the ones holding the map while I did the hard work of carrying the baby. I thought the goal was to keep me and my daughter healthy, yes, but also to make sure that when I walked out of that hospital, I still felt like a person.

The most significant gap was the complete absence of human connection amidst all the medical precision. They managed the "high-risk" label beautifully, but they failed the human being underneath. They kept my heart beating, but they didn’t care about what that heart was doing to my mind. 

I was treated like a broken machine that needed repair, not a terrified young woman facing her biggest fears alone. Every interaction was transactional—data points, charts, and monitors—while the trauma I was accumulating stayed ignored and untreated. They handed me a healthy baby and assumed their job was finished, but they left me trapped in the wreckage of a journey that I’m still struggling to survive today.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, I would tear down the wall that separates "clinical stability" from "emotional wellbeing." Right now, the system is designed to treat pregnancy and birth like a series of mechanical problems to be solved. If your blood pressure is stable, you’re "fixed." If the baby is breathing, you’re "successful." But that’s only half the story.

I would mandate that every high-risk pregnancy—especially for those of us who are young, alone, or carrying past trauma—comes with a dedicated, non-medical advocate or therapist integrated into the care team from day one. Someone whose job isn't to look at the monitors or the lab results, but to look at *me*. Someone to sit in the room so I’m not just listening to the beep of a pulse-oximeter, but hearing a human voice tell me I’m not crazy for being terrified.

The system treats "mental health" like an optional add-on that you only get if you break down completely. It shouldn't be an afterthought. Care needs to be holistic; it should treat the terror of the delivery as just as critical as the physical vitals. If the system saw the woman as the priority, not just the "case" or the "vessel," maybe I wouldn’t feel like I’m still standing in that delivery room months later. We need to be cared for as whole people, not just as risks to be managed.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

Thank you for actually listening. It’s strange, saying all this out loud—it makes it feel realer, but maybe a little less like a secret I have to keep locked away.

I think the one thing that gets lost in the medical shuffle is how much power we lose. When you’re in a hospital, especially when you're scared and you're alone, you’re not a participant in your own birth—you’re a passenger in your own body. I want people to know that the trauma doesn't just evaporate when you’re discharged. It follows you home. It’s there when you’re nursing, it’s there when you’re staring at the dark walls at night, and it’s there every time you feel a little bit of pain and think, *here it comes again.*

We need to stop telling women that a "healthy" baby means a "successful" birth. It’s not that simple. If the mother is left feeling shattered, or invisible, or like she survived a war instead of welcoming a child, that’s a failure of the system. I’m trying to find my way back to myself, day by day, but I wish I hadn’t had to fight the system just to feel like a human being while I was doing it. I just want to be seen as more than my blood pressure. That’s all. I hope this helps someone else—I really do.
